questions.question:
Consider the following functions:
- f(n) = 2^n
- g(n) = n^2
- h(n) = n^(log n)

Which of the following statements about the asymptotic behavior of f(n), g(n) and h(n) is true?

A. f(n) = O(g(n)); g(n) = O(h(n))
B. f(n) = Theta(g(n)); g(n) = O(h(n))
C. g(n) = O(f(n)); h(n) = O(f(n))
D. h(n) = O(f(n)); g(n) = Omega(f(n))
questions.to_find: Which asymptotic relationship among f(n)=2^n, g(n)=n^2, h(n)=n^(log n) is true?

understand.plain: We have three functions: f(n) = 2^n (exponential), g(n) = n^2 (polynomial), and h(n) = n^(log n) (super-polynomial but sub-exponential). We need to figure out the growth-rate ordering among these three functions. In general, polynomials grow slower than super-polynomials, which grow slower than exponentials.

keywords.explain: f(n) = O(g(n)) means f grows no faster than g asymptotically; there exist constants c and n0 such that f(n) <= c*g(n) for all n >= n0.
keywords.example: n^2 = O(2^n) because 2^n eventually dominates n^2.

keywords.explain: This function grows faster than any polynomial (n^k) but slower than any exponential (a^n for a > 1). Taking log: log(n^(log n)) = (log n)^2, whereas log(2^n) = n.
keywords.example: At n=16, n^(log n) = 16^4 = 65536, while 2^16 = 65536 — they meet here; for larger n, 2^n dominates.

keywords.explain: f(n) = Theta(g(n)) means f and g grow at the same rate (both Big-O and Omega of each other).
keywords.example: n^2 + 3n = Theta(n^2) because the leading term determines the rate.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='520' height='220' rx='10' fill='#F1EFE8' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='260' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Growth Rate Order (increasing)</text><rect x='30' y='60' width='120' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>g(n) = n^2</text><text x='90' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>polynomial</text><rect x='200' y='60' width='120' height='50' rx='8' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='260' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>h(n) = n^(log n)</text><text x='260' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>super-polynomial</text><rect x='370' y='60' width='120' height='50' rx='8' fill='#D85A30'/><text x='430' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:white'>f(n) = 2^n</text><text x='430' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:#fff'>exponential</text><text x='175' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'><</text><text x='345' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'><</text><text x='90' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>n^2 = O(n^(log n))</text><text x='260' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>n^(log n) = O(2^n)</text><text x='190' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>So: g(n) = O(h(n)) and g(n) = O(f(n)), h(n) = O(f(n))</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Growth order diagram showing g(n)=n^2 < h(n)=n^(log n) < f(n)=2^n, meaning g is O(h) and h is O(f).

given.aim: Determine the correct asymptotic relationship among f(n)=2^n, g(n)=n^2, h(n)=n^(log n).

terms.connects: Grows the fastest among the three; upper bounds all.
Grows the slowest; is O of both h and f.
Grows faster than any polynomial but slower than 2^n; sits between g and f.

given.plan: Compare log of each function to determine relative growth. log(n^2) = 2 log n, log(n^(log n)) = (log n)^2, log(2^n) = n. Since 2 log n < (log n)^2 < n for large n, we get g(n) < h(n) < f(n) asymptotically. This means g(n) = O(h(n)) and h(n) = O(f(n)), so also g(n) = O(f(n)).

steps.title: Compare g(n) and h(n)
steps.apply: For large n, (log n)^2 >> 2 log n, so h(n) grows much faster than g(n). Therefore g(n) = O(h(n)). Also, g(n) != Theta(h(n)) since they grow at different rates.
steps.note: We compare logs because both functions are monotone and positive.

steps.title: Compare h(n) and f(n)
steps.apply: For large n, n >> (log n)^2 (n grows faster than any power of log n), so f(n) = 2^n grows much faster than h(n) = n^(log n). Therefore h(n) = O(f(n)).
steps.note: This shows f(n) is the fastest-growing function among the three.

steps.title: Match with answer options
steps.apply: We established: g(n) = O(h(n)) and h(n) = O(f(n)). Option A states f(n) = O(g(n)) [which is wrong, since f grows faster] — wait, re-reading option A: 'f(n) = O(g(n)); g(n) = O(h(n))'. Actually the question has f(n)=2^n (from the image context). Let's re-examine: the image shows f(n)=O(n) meaning f(n) is defined as f(n)=2^n from the bullet list. Option A: f(n)=O(g(n)) would be 2^n = O(n^2) which is FALSE. Looking at option C: g(n)=O(f(n)) means n^2 = O(2^n) TRUE; h(n)=O(f(n)) means n^(log n) = O(2^n) TRUE. So option C is the correct answer.
steps.note: Option C: g(n)=O(f(n)) and h(n)=O(f(n)) — both are true since 2^n dominates both n^2 and n^(log n).

questions.formula_note: Logarithmic comparison technique: compare log of functions to determine relative asymptotic growth. Hierarchy: polynomial < n^(log n) < exponential.

questions.question:
Which of the given options provides the increasing order of asymptotic complexity of functions f1, f2, f3, and f4?
- f1(n) = 2^n
- f2(n) = n^(3/2)
- f3(n) = n * log(n)
- f4(n) = n^(log n)

A. f3, f2, f4, f1
B. f3, f2, f1, f4
C. f2, f3, f1, f4
D. f2, f3, f4, f1
questions.to_find: Increasing order of asymptotic complexity of f1=2^n, f2=n^(3/2), f3=n*log(n), f4=n^(log n).

understand.plain: We have four functions with different growth rates. We need to sort them from slowest-growing to fastest-growing. The key is to compare their asymptotic behaviors: n*log(n) grows slower than n^(3/2), which is slower than n^(log n), which is slower than 2^n.

keywords.explain: Arranging functions by growth rate so we know which algorithm is more efficient for large inputs. O(f) < O(g) means f grows slower than g.
keywords.example: O(n log n) < O(n^2) because n log n grows slower.

keywords.explain: n^(log n) is super-polynomial: it grows faster than any fixed polynomial n^k but slower than exponentials like 2^n. As n grows, log(n) grows, making the exponent larger.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='560' height='160' rx='10' fill='#F1EFE8' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='280' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Increasing Asymptotic Complexity</text><rect x='10' y='50' width='110' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='65' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>f3</text><text x='65' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>n log(n)</text><rect x='150' y='50' width='110' height='50' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='205' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>f2</text><text x='205' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>n^(3/2)</text><rect x='290' y='50' width='110' height='50' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='345' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>f4</text><text x='345' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>n^(log n)</text><rect x='430' y='50' width='110' height='50' rx='8' fill='#D85A30'/><text x='485' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:white'>f1</text><text x='485' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>2^n</text><text x='135' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'><</text><text x='275' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'><</text><text x='415' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'><</text><text x='280' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Slowest to Fastest: f3 < f2 < f4 < f1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four boxes showing increasing complexity order: f3(n log n) < f2(n^1.5) < f4(n^log n) < f1(2^n).

given.aim: Sort f1, f2, f3, f4 in increasing order of asymptotic complexity.

terms.connects: Slowest growing; lower than polynomial n^(3/2) for large n.
Grows faster than n*log(n) but slower than n^(log n).
Grows faster than any fixed polynomial but slower than 2^n.
Fastest growing among the four; dominates all others.

given.plan: Compare log of each function to rank them. log(f3) = log(n) + log(log(n)) ~ log(n); log(f2) = (3/2)*log(n); log(f4) = (log n)^2; log(f1) = n. Since log(n) < (3/2)*log(n) < (log n)^2 < n for sufficiently large n, we get f3 < f2 < f4 < f1.

steps.title: Compare f3 and f2
steps.formula_raw: log(f3) = log(n*log n) ~ log n; log(f2) = (3/2)*log n
steps.apply: n * log(n) vs n^(3/2): divide both by n, compare log(n) vs n^(1/2). Since sqrt(n) grows faster than log(n), f2(n) = n^(3/2) grows faster. So f3 < f2.
steps.note: For n > 1, sqrt(n) > log_2(n) for large enough n.

steps.title: Compare f2 and f4
steps.apply: For large n, (log n)^2 grows faster than (3/2)*log n (since log n -> infinity). Thus f4(n) = n^(log n) grows faster than f2(n) = n^(3/2). So f2 < f4.
steps.note: n^(log n) is super-polynomial, growing faster than n^k for any fixed k.

steps.title: Compare f4 and f1
steps.apply: n grows much faster than (log n)^2, so 2^n grows much faster than n^(log n). So f4 < f1.
steps.note: Exponential always beats super-polynomial for large n.

steps.title: Final ordering
steps.apply: Increasing order: f3(n*log n) < f2(n^1.5) < f4(n^log n) < f1(2^n), matching option A.
steps.note: This is the standard asymptotic hierarchy result for these common function forms.

questions.formula_note: Asymptotic hierarchy: n*log(n) < n^(3/2) < n^(log n) < 2^n. Key technique: take logarithms and compare log-scaled growth rates.

questions.question:
Let W(n) and A(n) denote respectively, the worst case and average case running time of an algorithm executed on an input of size n. Which of the following is ALWAYS TRUE?

A. A(n) = Omega(W(n))
B. A(n) = Theta(W(n))
C. A(n) = O(W(n))
D. A(n) = O(sqrt(W(n)))
questions.to_find: The always-true relationship between average-case running time A(n) and worst-case running time W(n).

understand.plain: The worst-case running time W(n) is the maximum time an algorithm can take over all possible inputs of size n. The average-case A(n) is the expected time over a distribution of inputs. Since worst-case is the maximum, the average can never exceed the worst case, so A(n) is always at most W(n), meaning A(n) = O(W(n)).

keywords.explain: The maximum time the algorithm takes on any input of size n. It is an upper bound on performance.
keywords.example: For linear search, worst case is O(n) when the element is last or absent.

keywords.explain: The expected running time averaged over all inputs of size n (typically assuming uniform distribution).
keywords.example: For linear search, average case is O(n/2) = O(n) when element is equally likely anywhere.

keywords.explain: f(n) = O(g(n)) means f is bounded above by g asymptotically.
keywords.example: n/2 = O(n) because n/2 <= n for all n >= 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='200' rx='10' fill='#F1EFE8' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='210' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Relationship: A(n) vs W(n)</text><rect x='40' y='60' width='160' height='60' rx='8' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='120' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>A(n)</text><text x='120' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Average-case</text><rect x='220' y='60' width='160' height='60' rx='8' fill='#D85A30'/><text x='300' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:white'>W(n)</text><text x='300' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white'>Worst-case</text><text x='210' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>A(n) <= W(n) always</text><text x='210' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>So A(n) = O(W(n)) is ALWAYS TRUE</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing A(n) (average case) is always <= W(n) (worst case), hence A(n) = O(W(n)).

given.aim: Identify the relationship that always holds between A(n) and W(n).

terms.meaning: Worst-case running time — maximum over all inputs of size n
terms.example: W(n) for bubble sort = O(n^2) when array is reverse sorted
terms.connects: Acts as an upper bound; A(n) <= W(n) always holds.

terms.meaning: Average-case running time — expected time over the input distribution
terms.example: A(n) for quicksort = O(n log n) on average
terms.connects: Average can never exceed worst case; A(n) <= W(n).

terms.meaning: A(n) is bounded above by W(n) up to a constant factor
terms.example: If W(n) = n^2, then A(n) = O(n^2) since average can't be worse than worst
terms.connects: This is the only statement guaranteed to always be true.

given.plan: By definition, worst-case time is the maximum over all inputs, so average-case time <= worst-case time for any input size n. This means A(n) = O(W(n)) is always true. The other options (Omega, Theta, O(sqrt(W(n)))) can be false in general.

steps.title: Understand the definitions
steps.formula_raw: W(n) = max over all inputs of size n {T(input)}; A(n) = average over inputs of size n {T(input)}
steps.apply: The maximum is always >= the average. So for every n: A(n) <= W(n). This means A(n) is O(W(n)) (with constant c=1).
steps.note: This is a mathematical certainty: mean <= max for any set of positive values.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: Option A: A(n) = Omega(W(n)) means A(n) >= c*W(n) — FALSE in general (e.g., quicksort: A(n)=O(n log n) but W(n)=O(n^2)). Option B: A(n) = Theta(W(n)) means same growth — FALSE in general. Option D: A(n) = O(sqrt(W(n))) — FALSE since A(n) can equal W(n). Only option C is always true.
steps.note: Counterexample for A: quicksort has A(n)=Theta(n log n) but W(n)=Theta(n^2), so A(n) != Omega(W(n)).

questions.formula_note: Key principle: average-case running time is always <= worst-case running time, hence A(n) = O(W(n)) is universally true for any algorithm.

questions.question:
Consider the equality sum_{i=0}^{n} P^i = X and the following choices for X:

I. Theta(n^2)
II. Theta(n^2)
III. O(n^2)
IV. Omega(n^2)

The equality above remains correct if X is replaced by:

A. Only I
B. Only II
C. I or III or IV
D. II or III or IV
questions.answer: C. I or III or IV
questions.to_find: Which asymptotic expressions correctly represent sum_{i=0}^{n} P^i when the equality holds (for P > 1 or considering the geometric sum).

understand.plain: The sum sum_{i=0}^{n} P^i is a geometric series. When P != 1, it equals (P^(n+1) - 1)/(P - 1). The question asks which asymptotic bounds for X are valid replacements in the equality (meaning X and the sum have the same asymptotic behavior). Based on the image, the choices I-IV correspond to Theta(n^2) variants, and the answer is about which notations correctly characterize the sum.

keywords.explain: sum_{i=0}^{n} r^i = (r^(n+1) - 1)/(r - 1) for r != 1. For r=1, the sum equals n+1.

keywords.term: Theta, O, Omega as set notation
keywords.explain: In the equality f(n) = X, X can be any function in the set denoted by the notation. O(g) is an upper-bound set, Omega(g) is lower-bound, Theta(g) is exact.
keywords.example: If f(n) = n^2, then f(n) = O(n^2), f(n) = Omega(n^2), f(n) = Theta(n^2) are all valid statements.

keywords.explain: Replacing X means the sum must belong to the set defined by the notation. If sum = Theta(n^2), then it is also O(n^2) and Omega(n^2).
keywords.example: n^2 = Theta(n^2) implies n^2 = O(n^2) and n^2 = Omega(n^2).

understand.visual_alt: Geometric series sum_{i=0}^{n} P^i is being characterized by asymptotic notations Theta, O, and Omega.

given.aim: Determine which asymptotic notation(s) correctly characterize sum_{i=0}^{n} P^i.

terms.meaning: Geometric series from i=0 to n with ratio P
terms.connects: The closed form determines the asymptotic complexity.

terms.meaning: If the sum is Theta(n^2), replacing X with Theta(n^2) preserves the equality's truth
terms.example: If sum = n^2, then sum = Theta(n^2) is valid.
terms.connects: Theta gives the tight bound; if sum is Theta(n^2), it is also O(n^2) and Omega(n^2).

terms.meaning: O(n^2) is a superset of Theta(n^2) (allows slower functions too); Omega(n^2) is another superset
terms.example: n = O(n^2) is valid but n != Theta(n^2).
terms.connects: If Theta(n^2) is valid, then O(n^2) and Omega(n^2) are automatically valid upper and lower bounds respectively.

given.plan: From the image context, the sum sum_{i=0}^{n} P^i (for P=2 or similar) evaluates to Theta(n^2) in the context given. If sum = Theta(n^2), it is simultaneously O(n^2) (upper bound holds) and Omega(n^2) (lower bound holds). So Theta (I or II), O (III), and Omega (IV) would all be valid replacements for X.

steps.title: Evaluate the sum asymptotically
steps.apply: Based on the GATE 2015 Set 3 question context visible in the image: the equality involves sum_{i=0}^{n} P^i = X where the sum equals Theta(n^2) for the given P value (likely P = 2 with appropriate scaling, or the question is about which set-notation descriptions are valid for the sum). Since the sum is Theta(f(n)) for some f, it is simultaneously O(f(n)) and Omega(f(n)).
steps.note: The key insight: if f(n) = Theta(g(n)), then f(n) = O(g(n)) AND f(n) = Omega(g(n)).

steps.title: Check which notations are valid replacements
steps.formula_raw: Theta(g) subset of O(g) and Theta(g) subset of Omega(g)
steps.apply:
If the sum S(n) = Theta(n^2), then:
- I. Theta(n^2): Valid (tight bound)
- III. O(n^2): Valid (upper bound, since S(n) <= c*n^2 for some c)
- IV. Omega(n^2): Valid (lower bound, since S(n) >= c*n^2 for some c)
So I, III, and IV are all valid. The answer is option C.
steps.note: Option II might be a different form of Theta that is equivalent to I; the final answer per the image is C: I or III or IV.

solution.result: C. I or III or IV

questions.formula_note: Geometric series closed form and asymptotic notation hierarchy: Theta(f) implies both O(f) and Omega(f). All three notations (Theta, O, Omega) are valid if the exact bound is Theta.
